title: GreenLoop Employee Handbook 2025
text:
GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Employee Handbook 2025 | | HR-HBK-2025-v1.4

SECTION 1
Company profile, mission, and values

GreenLoop Robotics designs and operates intelligent waste-sorting systems for universities,
transport hubs, offices, hospitals, and retail sites. The company combines edge AI,
electromechanical routing, and a cloud operations platform.

Operating footprint

GreenLoop was founded in 2021. The company maintains an administrative office and product center in
Amman, an engineering and validation laboratory in Ramallah, and regional field teams that serve customer
sites. At the end of Q3 FY2025, the workforce was 148 employees.

Mission

Our mission is to make correct waste separation easy, measurable, and scalable. Product decisions must
balance environmental impact, user safety, classification quality, serviceability, and responsible handling of
customer data.

Values

● | Evidence before confidence: measure performance and state uncertainty clearly.

● | Design for the field: laboratory success matters only when systems work reliably at customer sites.

● | Own the full loop: document decisions, monitor outcomes, and correct failures.

● | Respect people and data: protect colleagues, customers, bystanders, and confidential information.

● | Improve in small releases: prefer reversible, tested changes over unreviewed large changes.

No employee may present laboratory accuracy as guaranteed field accuracy. Performance
claims must include the test conditions and the approved source.

SECTION 2
Employment principles and responsibilities

Every employee is responsible for safe work, accurate records, confidentiality, respectful
conduct, and timely escalation of risks. Managers are responsible for applying policy
consistently and documenting exceptions.

Employment categories

Category | Typical arrangement | Policy notes

Regular full-time | 40 hours per week | Eligible for standard benefits and leave

Regular part-time | 20 to 32 hours per week | Benefits prorated where local law permits

Fixed-term | Project or time limited | End date stated in signed agreement

Intern | Supervised learning placement | No unsupervised lab or customer access

Contractor | Services agreement | Access limited to assigned systems and term

Employee responsibilities

● | Keep personal, payroll, emergency, and contact information current.

● | Complete security, safety, privacy, and conduct training by assigned deadlines.

● | Record working time and project costs accurately when the role requires it.

● | Ask for clarification before performing work outside authorization or competence.

● | Report suspected misconduct, unsafe conditions, data exposure, or product risk without delay.

Manager responsibilities

Managers approve work modes, access, travel, overtime, and exceptions only within their delegated
authority. A manager may not waive security controls, bypass laboratory safety procedures, or conceal a
quality event to meet a delivery target.

SECTION 3
Work modes and role eligibility

Work mode is based on the duties of the role, not seniority. The signed job profile and the
matrix below establish the default. Managers may require temporary onsite attendance for
launches, audits, incidents, training, or customer commitments.

Default rule: software and AI employees may work remotely up to three days per week;
hardware and validation employees normally work onsite four days per week.

Role group | Maximum remote | Minimum onsite expectation | Approval
days

Software, AI, data, design | 3 per week | Tuesday and Thursday | Manager schedule
approval

Finance, people, marketing | 3 per week | Tuesday and Thursday | Manager schedule
approval

Customer success and | 2 per week | Coverage roster controls | Team lead approval
support

Hardware and validation | 1 per week | 4 laboratory days | Lab manager approval

Field service | As roster permits | Customer-site schedule | Regional operations
approval

Interns in first 30 days | 0 by default | Onsite onboarding | Department head
exception

Remote work is a location, not a reduction in availability

Remote-eligible employees remain responsible for assigned outcomes, customer commitments, core
collaboration hours, security controls, and a professional working environment. Remote days cannot be
accumulated, transferred to another week, or treated as leave.

Exceptions

● | A documented medical accommodation follows the accommodation process and may differ from this matrix.

● | A temporary project exception requires a start date, end date, business reason, and manager approval.

GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Employee Handbook 2025 | | HR-HBK-2025-v1.4

SECTION 4
Weekly schedules and collaboration hours

GreenLoop uses flexible start and finish times while protecting predictable overlap for
cross-functional work. The standard workweek is Sunday through Thursday for regional teams
unless a local contract states otherwise.

Core collaboration window

All employees must normally be reachable from 10:00 to 15:00 Asia/Hebron on scheduled working days. Field
assignments, approved leave, customer maintenance windows, and documented accommodations are valid
exceptions.

Planning expectations

● | Publish the weekly work location in the team calendar by 16:00 Thursday.

● | Schedule focused work outside the core window when practical.

● | Do not schedule recurring meetings before 09:30 or after 16:30 without team consent.

● | Use the support roster, not personal availability, for after-hours customer coverage.

● | Obtain written approval before overtime where local law or contract requires it.

Attendance

Repeated unavailability during core hours, failure to attend required laboratory work, or inaccurate location
reporting may result in a work-mode review. A single connectivity failure is not misconduct when promptly
communicated and reasonably mitigated.

Tuesday and Thursday are common onsite collaboration days for remote-eligible teams.
Hardware and validation schedules include four laboratory days each week.

GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Employee Handbook 2025 | | HR-HBK-2025-v1.4

SECTION 5
Remote workspace and company equipment

A remote workspace must support confidential conversation, safe equipment use, stable
connectivity, and reliable participation. Company equipment remains GreenLoop property and
may be inspected, updated, or recovered.

Standard equipment

Item | Standard | Employee responsibility

Laptop | Managed, encrypted corporate device | Do not share or disable controls

Authentication key | FIDO2 key where assigned | Keep separate from unattended laptop

Headset | Noise-reducing headset | Use for confidential calls

Home office allowance | Up to USD 600 at hire | Submit receipts within 60 days

Internet support | Up to USD 40 per month | Maintain a commercially reasonable connection

Minimum workspace controls

● | Lock the screen whenever the device is unattended.

● | Prevent family members, visitors, and housemates from using company devices.

● | Use a privacy screen when confidential content may be visible to others.

● | Do not discuss customer incidents in cafes, public transport, or shared public rooms.

● | Store hardware samples, access cards, and printed material in a locked location.

Loss or damage

Lost, stolen, or potentially accessed equipment must be reported to the Security Hotline within 30 minutes of
discovery. Employees must not delay reporting while attempting to locate a device.

title: GreenLoop Employee Handbook 2025
text:
GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Employee Handbook 2025 | | HR-HBK-2025-v1.4

SECTION 6
Information security and identity controls

Security controls apply in every work location. No manager may approve bypassing multi-factor
authentication, endpoint management, encryption, least privilege, or incident reporting
requirements.

Remote access to internal systems requires a managed company device, multi-factor
authentication, and the GreenLoop VPN.

Required controls

● | Use only the assigned identity; shared accounts are prohibited except approved service accounts.

● | Use multi-factor authentication for company and customer systems.

● | Connect through the GreenLoop VPN when accessing internal engineering, finance, or customer support
systems remotely.

● | Install updates within the endpoint management deadline; critical updates may be forced immediately.

● | Use the company password manager and unique credentials for every service.

● | Approve access requests only when the business need and requested duration are clear.

Privileged access

Production, model deployment, customer image review, and finance administration use time-bound privileged
roles. Privileged actions are logged and reviewed. Engineers must use a separate administrative identity
when instructed by Information Security.

Prohibited actions

Employees may not disable endpoint protection, send credentials through chat, copy tokens into tickets,
expose local development ports to the internet, or use unapproved remote desktop tools. Suspected
credential disclosure requires immediate password rotation and incident reporting.

SECTION 7
Data classification and customer images

Information must be labeled and handled according to sensitivity. Customer camera images can
contain people, badges, documents, or location details and are treated as Restricted unless an
approved de-identification process changes the classification.

Class | Examples | Minimum handling

Public | Published product pages and approved press | Approved public channel
releases

Internal | Team procedures and non-sensitive project plans | Company account only

Confidential | Contracts, forecasts, employee records | Need-to-know access and encryption

Restricted | Customer images, credentials, security logs | Approved systems, logging, time-bound access

Customer image rules

● | Do not download customer images to personal devices or personal cloud storage.

● | Use the controlled review console when investigating classification quality.

● | Do not include identifiable customer images in presentations, demos, or model experiments without written
approval.

● | Record the ticket, purpose, dataset, and retention period for every approved export.

● | Delete approved working copies when the documented task is complete.

Paper records

Printing Confidential or Restricted information requires a business need. Printed records must be collected
immediately, stored in a locked cabinet, and destroyed using approved secure disposal.

SECTION 8
Acceptable use and generative AI

Company systems are provided for legitimate work. Limited personal use is acceptable when
lawful, infrequent, secure, and not disruptive. GreenLoop may log and monitor company
systems as allowed by law and policy.

Generative AI rules

● | Use only AI services listed in the approved tools register.

● | Do not submit Restricted data, customer images, credentials, source code marked restricted, or non-public
financial data to public AI services.

● | Review generated code, calculations, citations, and factual claims before use.

● | Label AI-assisted customer content when the review procedure requires disclosure.

● | A human owner remains accountable for every AI-assisted decision and deliverable.

Software and storage

Install software through the managed catalog or obtain Security approval. Personal email, consumer
file-sharing services, unapproved browser extensions, and removable media must not be used for company
data.

Testing and simulation

Security testing, load testing, scanning, and adversarial model evaluation require a defined scope and system
owner approval. Testing customer environments without explicit authorization is prohibited.

Public AI tools must never receive customer images or unpublished Q3 financial information.

title: GreenLoop Employee Handbook 2025
text:
GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Employee Handbook 2025 | | HR-HBK-2025-v1.4

SECTION 9
Communication, meetings, and
documentation

GreenLoop is documentation-first. Decisions that affect customers, product behavior, safety,
finance, or policy must be recorded in the designated system and cannot remain only in chat or
a meeting recording.

Channel guidance

Channel | Use | Not suitable for

Project record | Requirements, decisions, owners, evidence | Casual conversation

Ticketing system | Incidents, support cases, defects | Untracked policy approval

Chat | Coordination and quick questions | Final decisions or Restricted data

Video meeting | Ambiguous, sensitive, or collaborative topics | Avoiding written follow-up

Email | External formal communication | Credentials or large customer datasets

Meeting standard

● | Publish an agenda and required decision before the meeting.

● | Invite only people needed to decide, advise, or execute.

● | Record decisions, dissent, owner, and due date in the source system.

● | Do not record meetings by default; obtain consent and define retention when recording is necessary.

Asynchronous work

A useful async update states the outcome, evidence, uncertainty, decision requested, owner, and deadline.
Employees should not create artificial urgency by sending the same request across several channels.

GreenLoop is documentation-first. Decisions that affect customers, product behavior, safety,
finance, or policy must be recorded in the designated system and cannot remain only in chat or
a meeting recording.

Channel guidance

Channel | Use | Not suitable for

Project record | Requirements, decisions, owners, evidence | Casual conversation

Ticketing system | Incidents, support cases, defects | Untracked policy approval

Chat | Coordination and quick questions | Final decisions or Restricted data

Video meeting | Ambiguous, sensitive, or collaborative topics | Avoiding written follow-up

Email | External formal communication | Credentials or large customer datasets

Meeting standard

● | Publish an agenda and required decision before the meeting.

● | Invite only people needed to decide, advise, or execute.

● | Record decisions, dissent, owner, and due date in the source system.

● | Do not record meetings by default; obtain consent and define retention when recording is necessary.

Asynchronous work

A useful async update states the outcome, evidence, uncertainty, decision requested, owner, and deadline.
Employees should not create artificial urgency by sending the same request across several channels.

Leave and time away

transfer required access without sharing credentials. Managers must distribute coverage fairly and must not
require routine availability during approved leave.

Unplanned absence

Employees should use the emergency contact path when they cannot access normal systems. Health details
should be shared only with People and Culture or as required by local law, not broadly with the team.

SECTION 11
Performance and professional development

Performance is evaluated through outcomes, quality, collaboration, safety, and values. Physical
presence alone is not a performance measure, and remote employees are evaluated using the
same role expectations as onsite peers.

Review cycle

● | Quarterly check-in: priorities, evidence, support, and development actions.

● | Midyear review: progress against role outcomes and behavior expectations.

● | Annual review: sustained impact, capability, values, and compensation input.

● | Project retrospective: lessons from releases, incidents, field deployments, and audits.

Evidence

Useful evidence includes validated product metrics, customer outcomes, quality improvements, incident
learning, delivery reliability, documentation, mentoring, and effective risk escalation. Unsupported claims,
activity volume without outcomes, or concealed defects are not positive performance evidence.

Learning support

Regular employees may receive an annual development budget of up to USD 1,200 with manager approval.
Training must connect to a current or planned capability. Employees who attend external training share
relevant learning without disclosing licensed material.

A product target is not an achieved result. Reviews must distinguish planned, laboratory,
pilot, and field performance.

SECTION 13
Conduct, inclusion, and respectful work

GreenLoop prohibits discrimination, harassment, retaliation, bullying, threats, and humiliating
conduct. The policy applies in offices, laboratories, customer sites, travel, chat, video calls,
social events, and work-related online spaces.

Expected conduct

● | Challenge ideas with evidence without attacking people.

● | Use names and forms of address requested by colleagues.

● | Provide accessible materials and reasonable notice for participation.

● | Do not circulate offensive content or use customer data as entertainment.

● | Intervene safely or report conduct that may harm another person.

Reporting options

Concerns may be raised with a manager, People and Culture, the Ethics Channel, or any executive. An
employee does not need to confront the person involved before reporting. Retaliation for a good-faith report
or participation in an investigation is prohibited.

Confidentiality

Investigations are handled as privately as practical, but absolute confidentiality cannot be promised.
Information is shared only with people who need it to assess, investigate, decide, or comply with law.

GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Employee Handbook 2025 | | HR-HBK-2025-v1.4

SECTION 14
Engineering laboratory safety

Only trained and authorized personnel may operate energized prototypes, lifting devices,
battery systems, cutting tools, or moving sorter assemblies. Remote work approval never
authorizes unsupervised laboratory work at home.

Core controls

● | Complete the task risk assessment before first use or after a material change.

● | Use lockout and tagout before entering a guarded mechanical area.

● | Verify isolation with an approved instrument before touching conductors.

● | Wear required eye, hand, hearing, and footwear protection.

● | Use a spotter for heavy modules and follow the posted lifting limit.

● | Keep aisles, emergency stops, fire equipment, and electrical panels clear.

Prototype control

Every prototype has an owner, revision, hazard label, test plan, and status. A unit marked NOT FOR FIELD USE
may not be demonstrated to a customer. Guards and emergency stops cannot be removed to accelerate a
test.

Near misses

A near miss is reported with the same urgency as an injury when a small change could have caused harm.
Work pauses until the immediate hazard is controlled. Reporting a near miss in good faith is expected
behavior, not failure.

Hardware and validation employees normally work in the controlled laboratory four days per
week because energized, mechanical, and waste-contact testing cannot be performed safely
as ordinary remote work.

SECTION 15
Customer and field-site safety

Customer rules and GreenLoop rules both apply at field sites. When they differ, employees
follow the safer lawful control and contact Regional Operations before proceeding.

Before work

● | Confirm site contact, scope, access, isolation, waste handling, and emergency arrangements.

● | Review recent service history and open hazards.

● | Inspect tools, test instruments, PPE, and replacement parts.

● | Establish a safe work zone that separates the public from moving equipment.

During work

Stop the sorter, isolate energy, and prevent automatic restart before clearing a jam or opening a guarded
area. Suspected sharp, biological, chemical, pressurized, hot, or electrical waste is not handled by GreenLoop
staff unless a specific approved procedure exists.

Completion

The technician restores guards, tests emergency stops, removes tools and waste, records changes, and
obtains site acknowledgement. A unit with an unresolved safety defect remains out of service and is labeled
accordingly.

SECTION 16
Incident reporting and business continuity

Fast reporting protects people and customers. Employees report facts known at the time and
clearly label assumptions. No employee is expected to complete an investigation before raising
an alert.

Event | Initial notification | Primary channel

Injury, fire, exposed energy | Immediately after emergency action | Site emergency process and Safety
Hotline

Lost device or credential | Within 30 minutes of discovery | Security Hotline

Suspected customer data exposure | Within 30 minutes | Security Incident channel

Product safety defect | Immediately and before further use | Quality escalation

Major service outage | Within 15 minutes | Operations incident channel

Conduct concern | As soon as reasonably practical | Manager, People, or Ethics Channel

Continuity priorities

The order of priority is life safety, containment, customer communication, service restoration, evidence
preservation, and learning. Teams use approved backups and failover procedures; they do not create
unreviewed shortcuts that increase security or safety risk.

Post-incident review

Reviews focus on system conditions, detection, decisions, and prevention. Records identify what is known,
what remains uncertain, corrective owners, due dates, and verification evidence.

SECTION 17
Conflicts, gifts, and ethical decisions

Employees act in GreenLoop's legitimate interests and disclose personal interests that could
affect, or appear to affect, judgment. Disclosure is required before participating in the relevant
decision.

Examples requiring disclosure

● | A financial interest in a supplier, customer, or competitor.

● | Hiring, supervising, or selecting a close relative or household member.

● | Outside work that overlaps with GreenLoop products, customers, or working time.

● | Use of company information, equipment, or contacts for a private venture.

● | A personal relationship with a bidder or audit subject.

Gifts and hospitality

Cash and cash equivalents are prohibited. Modest business hospitality may be accepted when lawful,
infrequent, transparent, and not connected to a pending decision. Gifts above USD 75 require written Ethics
approval; stricter customer or government rules prevail.

Accurate records

Employees may not create false invoices, time entries, test results, quality records, expense claims, forecasts,
or customer statements. Pressure to change or omit a material fact must be reported.

SECTION 18
Corrective action and employee concerns

GreenLoop aims to correct problems fairly, promptly, and proportionately. Depending on
severity, history, law, and risk, action may include coaching, a written expectation, access
restriction, reassignment, final warning, or termination.

Serious matters

The company may move directly to a serious corrective step for violence, harassment, retaliation, deliberate
safety bypass, theft, falsification, intentional data disclosure, unauthorized production access, or other
significant misconduct.

Raising a concern

● | State the decision, behavior, or condition being challenged.

● | Provide dates, records, witnesses, or other available evidence.

● | Explain the requested correction or protection.

● | Use People and Culture or the Ethics Channel if the manager is involved.

Good-faith protection

A report can be good faith even when it is not substantiated. Knowingly false statements or evidence
manipulation are misconduct, but an honest mistake or incomplete information is not automatically a policy
violation.

SECTION 1
Product purpose and approved use cases

The GreenLoop Sorter X1 is an indoor, attended waste-sorting station that identifies a single
deposited item, predicts one of five material classes, and routes the item to a corresponding
internal bin.

Approved environments

● | University and corporate campuses.

● | Airport and transport waiting areas outside security-critical baggage flows.

● | Shopping centers, food courts, and event venues.

● | Hospitals in public areas for ordinary non-clinical waste only.

● | Municipal demonstration and education sites.

Primary outcomes

The system aims to improve separation consistency, measure material volumes, reduce manual inspection,
and provide operators with fill, health, and classification analytics. It is not a hazardous-waste identifier and
does not certify regulatory compliance of the customer's waste stream.

Intended operator

Routine use requires no technical training. Installation, guard removal, jam clearing inside the mechanism,
calibration, and electrical service are restricted to trained personnel.

The X1 is designed for one ordinary item per deposit. It is not approved for medical, chemical,
hot, sharp, pressurized, radioactive, or industrial hazardous waste.

text:
GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Sorter X1 Product Specification | | PRD-GLX1-2025-v2.1

SECTION 3
System architecture

The X1 separates sensing, classification, routing, safety, and cloud functions so that a cloud
interruption does not prevent ordinary local sorting.

Layer | Primary components | Failure behavior

Interaction | Deposit door, indicator ring, display | Prompts user to wait or retry

Sensing | RGB camera, time-of-flight, weight, door sensors | Rejects uncertain cycle

Edge intelligence | GL Edge controller and GL-Vision model | Continues offline with cached policy

Routing | Rotary diverter, position encoder, bin doors | Stops safely on mismatch

Safety | Interlocks, emergency stop, overcurrent protection | Removes motion command

Cloud | Telemetry, fleet health, analytics, user management | Queues local events for later upload

Control principle

A sort cycle is accepted only when item presence, door state, classification confidence, diverter position, and
receiving-bin availability meet the active policy. The controller records reason codes for rejected or fallback
cycles.

Offline operation

The unit can classify and route for up to 72 hours without cloud connectivity. Dashboard data, remote
commands, and fleet configuration changes are unavailable until the connection is restored.

SECTION 4
Physical dimensions and materials

The Rev B cabinet is designed for indoor public use and service access from the rear.
Dimensions exclude optional signage and required service clearance.

Characteristic | Specification

Overall size | 1180 mm wide x 760 mm deep x 1760 mm high

Empty mass | 186 kg without removable bins

Installed mass | Up to 245 kg depending on configuration

Frame | Powder-coated galvanized steel

User surfaces | Flame-retardant ABS and stainless steel contact trim

Service clearance | 800 mm rear and 500 mm on each side

Anchoring | Four M10 anchors into verified substrate

Ingress and cleaning

The cabinet is splash resistant for normal indoor cleaning but is not assigned an outdoor IP rating.
High-pressure water, steam, hose cleaning, and floor flooding can damage sensors, seals, and electrical
assemblies.

Finish

Standard colors are GreenLoop Deep Green and neutral gray. Customer graphics may not cover ventilation,
labels, cameras, door edges, emergency controls, or service access.

SECTION 5
Power and environmental limits

Site conditions strongly influence reliability and classification performance. The installer records
voltage, protective earth, temperature, lighting, network, and floor conditions during
commissioning.

Parameter | Required range | Notes

Input power | 110 to 240 VAC, 50/60 Hz | Dedicated grounded outlet

Typical consumption | 95 W | Idle with cloud connected

Peak consumption | 250 W | During routing and display peak

Operating temperature | 5 C to 40 C | Indoor conditioned environment

Relative humidity | 20% to 80% | Non-condensing

Ambient light | 300 to 1200 lux | Avoid direct glare into camera

Floor slope | Less than 2 degrees | Rigid, level, anchored surface

Acoustic level | Below 60 dBA at 1 m | Normal sort cycle

Power loss

Without the optional UPS, a power loss stops the mechanism and preserves the event journal. The optional
UPS supports controlled shutdown and up to 20 minutes of telemetry and non-routing operation.

Classification outside the specified lighting range may remain functional but is not covered by
the laboratory accuracy specification.

SECTION 6
Sensors and item detection

The sensing chamber combines visual, distance, weight, door, and position signals. No single
sensor is treated as sufficient evidence that a valid item is ready for classification.

Sensor | Purpose | Nominal capability

RGB camera | Material and appearance features | 1920 x 1080 at controlled exposure

Time-of-flight | Item envelope and chamber occupancy | 20 to 600 mm working range

Load cell | Item presence and gross mass | 20 g to 2.5 kg operating range

Ultrasonic fill sensors | Remaining bin capacity | Five independent channels

Door interlocks | Safe state confirmation | Dual-channel service-door monitoring

Diverter encoder | Target position confirmation | Absolute position with tolerance check

Capture sequence

After the user closes the deposit door, the controller verifies stability, captures a short image burst, selects
the best frame, obtains weight and envelope features, and then invokes the model. Unstable or oversized
items are rejected before routing.

Calibration

Camera exposure and reference color are checked during commissioning and after camera replacement.
Load-cell zero is checked daily and calibrated quarterly or after mechanical work.

Edge compute and software baseline

The edge controller hosts the real-time state machine, classification runtime, local database,

Component | Controlled baseline

Edge compute | 8 GB industrial controller with 12 TOPS inference accelerator

Storage | 128 GB encrypted industrial SSD

Operating system | GreenLoop Edge OS 5.2 LTS

Controller firmware | X1 Control 2.8.3

Model runtime | GL Runtime 3.4

Local event buffer | Minimum 72 hours at rated daily volume

Update method | Signed A/B image with automatic rollback

Update policy

Security fixes may be applied during the customer's approved maintenance window. Model updates are
staged to a pilot group, monitored against quality gates, and expanded only after Product Quality approval.

Rollback

A failed health check after update returns the unit to the last known good image. Local maintenance may
force rollback only with an authorized service credential and a recorded ticket.

SECTION 8
Classification model and supported classes

GL-Vision 3.4.1 predicts five operational classes. The class describes the route configured for
the site; it is not a chemical analysis of material composition.

Class | Representative items | Important exclusions

Paper | Office paper, clean cartons, paper sleeves | Wet or heavily food-soiled fiber

Plastic | Bottles, cups, rigid containers | Film tangles and mixed laminates

Metal | Aluminum cans, small clean tins | Batteries and pressurized containers

Organic | Food scraps and compostable residue | Clinical or animal waste

Other | Unknown, mixed, low-confidence items | Does not imply safe general disposal

Confidence policy

A site policy maps prediction and confidence to a route. Predictions below the approved confidence threshold
route to Other or request user retry. Customers may not lower the threshold below the controlled minimum
without a documented pilot.

Model scope

The production model was trained and validated on ordinary indoor consumer waste. It is not designed for
industrial scrap, medical waste, powders, liquids without containers, or objects larger than the input envelope.

SECTION 9
Laboratory performance specification

Laboratory metrics were measured on the controlled GL-X1-B validation set using single, clean,
non-overlapping items, specified lighting, a clean lens, and calibrated sensors.

Metric | Specification | Test condition

Macro classification accuracy | 93.2% | Five-class controlled validation set

Paper accuracy | 96.1% | Clean representative items

Plastic accuracy | 94.0% | Rigid plastic mix

Metal accuracy | 95.2% | Cans and small tins

Organic accuracy | 91.0% | Representative food waste

Other accuracy | 89.7% | Mixed approved unknown set

P95 decision time | 1.8 seconds or less | From stable capture to route decision

Interpretation

The 93.2% result is a laboratory macro accuracy, not a guarantee for every item or customer site. Field
accuracy depends on waste mix, lighting, cleanliness, user behavior, installation, maintenance, and active
software.

Do not compare a field metric with the 93.2% laboratory value without describing the
different conditions.

SECTION 10
Input rules and multi-item handling

The X1 accepts one item at a time. The chamber uses visual and depth checks to detect
obvious multiple objects, but reliable separation of overlapping or bundled items is outside the
controlled product claim.

Accepted envelope

Parameter | Limit

Maximum item dimensions | 350 x 250 x 200 mm

Maximum item mass | 2.5 kg

Minimum reliable mass | 20 g

Recommended spacing | 5 seconds between users

Deposit rule | One unbagged item per cycle

Multiple items

When multiple objects are detected with high confidence, the user is prompted to remove and reinsert one
item. Overlapping items can appear as a single mixed object. If not detected, they may be routed to Other or
occasionally to an incorrect material bin.

Bags and bundles

Closed bags, nested cups, compressed bundles, strings, and long flexible film are not supported. These inputs
can hide material, exceed the envelope, wrap around moving parts, or create misleading visual features.

Known limitation: multi-item classification errors are possible. The product specification does
not assign a financial loss value to those errors.

SECTION 11
Mechanical routing and safety

A rotary diverter aligns the sensing chamber with one of five receiving bins. Motion is allowed
only when covers and service doors are closed, the path is clear, and a valid target position is
available.

Cycle sequence

● | Lock the user deposit door.

● | Confirm stable item and safe interlock state.

● | Classify and select receiving bin.

● | Move diverter and verify encoder position.

● | Release item and confirm chamber clear.

● | Return diverter to ready position and unlock deposit door.

Safety functions

Function | Behavior

Emergency stop | Removes motion command and requires manual reset

Service-door interlock | Prevents automatic motion while open

Motor overcurrent | Stops motion and records jam fault

Position mismatch | Stops and blocks new deposits

Restart after power loss | Requires self-test before user-ready state

Jam clearing

Only trained service personnel may open the guarded mechanism. Energy isolation is required before hands
or tools enter the routing volume.

SECTION 12
Bin capacity and fill monitoring

Five removable 60-liter bins provide a nominal total volume of 300 liters. Actual usable volume
depends on item shape, compaction, liners, and local waste practice.

Fill estimation

Each bin uses an ultrasonic distance channel and a model that converts several readings into an estimated
percentage. Readings are filtered to reduce false alerts from irregular item surfaces.

Threshold | Default action

70% | Dashboard advisory

85% | Service notification

95% | High-priority collection request

Fill estimation

Each bin uses an ultrasonic distance channel and a model that converts several readings into an estimated

95% | High-priority collection request

Service access

opened. Operators verify liner position and sensor clearance after replacement.

Fill percentage is an operational estimate with a typical error of plus or minus 8 percentage points for ordinary
mixed shapes. It is not a trade-certified volume or weight measurement.

● | Idle prompt invites one item.

● | Door-open state shows placement guidance.

● | Processing state asks the next user to wait.

● | Result state displays predicted class and destination color.

● | Retry state explains multiple, oversized, or uncertain input.

● | Unavailable state directs the user to an ordinary waste station.

Accessibility

Default text meets the product contrast target, essential controls are reachable from the front approach,
sound can be adjusted or disabled, and icons accompany class colors. Site signage and physical approach
remain the customer's responsibility.

Languages

The standard release supports English and Arabic. Additional language packs require translation review,
layout verification, and customer approval before deployment.

SECTION 15
Security architecture

Security uses device identity, encrypted communication, signed software, least privilege,
tenant separation, audit logging, and monitored cloud services.

Control | Baseline

Data in transit | TLS 1.3 for supported cloud connections

Data at rest | AES-256 or equivalent managed encryption

Device identity | Unique certificate stored in hardware-backed key storage

User authentication | MFA; SAML or OIDC SSO in Pro

Authorization | Role-based access with tenant and site scope

Software integrity | Signed updates with verified boot chain

Audit retention | Minimum 24 months for administrative events

Vulnerability handling | Risk-ranked remediation and customer advisory process

Secrets

API secrets and device credentials are not displayed after creation. Customer administrators can revoke user
sessions and rotate API keys. GreenLoop support does not request passwords through email or chat.

Shared responsibility

Customers control user approval, SSO settings, site network rules, physical access, exports, and local
retention choices. GreenLoop controls platform operation, device software signing, tenant separation, and
service monitoring.

and must be stored only in approved systems.

customer images on personal devices.

SECTION 17
Network and deployment requirements

A wired Ethernet connection is preferred. Wi-Fi or cellular may be used when signal, security,
data allowance, and local policy meet the site design.

Requirement | Minimum

Bandwidth | 2 Mbps down and 1 Mbps up average per unit

Latency | Less than 250 ms preferred for dashboard responsiveness

Addressing | DHCP reservation or documented static configuration

Outbound access | HTTPS 443 and approved time synchronization

Inbound access | No public inbound port required

Time | Authenticated or approved NTP source

Segmentation | Dedicated IoT or managed device network recommended

Proxy and inspection

TLS interception can break certificate-based device authentication and requires engineering review. The
customer provides proxy details and certificates before commissioning.

Network loss

Sorting continues locally for up to 72 hours. The unit displays a service indicator after the configured
connectivity threshold and uploads queued events when service returns.

SECTION 20
Site readiness and installation

Installation begins only after the customer and GreenLoop confirm structural, electrical,
network, access, cleaning, waste, and safety readiness.

Readiness checklist

● | Level structural floor and approved anchor points.

● | Dedicated grounded outlet within cable reach.

● | Network path tested with required destinations.

● | Specified lighting without direct glare.

● | Rear and side service clearances available.

● | Waste collection plan and bin liners ready.

● | Public flow does not block emergency egress.

● | Named customer administrator and site operator available.

Installation

Trained installers inspect shipping condition, move and anchor the cabinet, connect power and network,
verify protective earth, install bins, load the approved configuration, and perform safety and functional tests.

Handover

The customer receives serial records, site photos, acceptance results, operator training, admin access,
maintenance schedule, and open-item list. A unit with a failed safety test is not handed over for public use.

Every 14 days | Clean lens and chamber; inspect liners and sensors | Site operator

Quarterly | Load-cell calibration and full safety test | GreenLoop certified
technician

Annually | Electrical inspection, wear parts, anchors, and battery if fitted | GreenLoop certified
technician

areas. The unit must be placed in maintenance mode before chamber or bin cleaning.

and follow-up. Missing preventive maintenance can exclude a failure from the service-level calculation.

SECTION 23

message while detailed codes remain in the service interface.

Fault family | Typical response

Door or interlock | Block motion; inspect closure and sensor

Diverter position | Stop cycle; service inspection required

Camera quality | Clean lens; verify light and calibration

Weight instability | Remove item; inspect chamber and load cell

Bin full | Disable or redirect route according to policy

Network offline | Continue local sorting and queue events

Storage threshold | Prioritize upload and restrict image sampling

Remote diagnostics

Authorized support can view health, recent fault codes, software versions, and sanitized logs. Remote support
access is time bound and audited. Safety resets that require physical inspection cannot be completed
remotely.

Evidence preservation

Do not repeatedly clear a fault before logs are captured. For recurring classification issues, record site
lighting, lens condition, item type, model version, confidence, route, and whether multiple items were
present.

SECTION 24
Service levels and support

X1 Pro includes a monthly 99.5% availability target for the customer-facing cloud dashboard.
Device local sorting availability and customer network availability are measured separately.

Severity | Example | Initial response target

P1 Critical | Safety risk or fleet-wide cloud outage | 30 minutes, 24x7

P2 High | Site unable to sort with no workaround | 2 business hours

P3 Medium | Degraded analytics or repeated non-safety fault | 8 business hours

P4 Low | Question, report, or cosmetic issue | 2 business days

Dashboard SLA

Availability equals eligible minutes minus unavailable eligible minutes, divided by eligible minutes. Planned
maintenance announced at least 72 hours ahead, customer network failure, customer configuration, force
majeure, and misuse are excluded as defined in the order.

Service credits

Eligible customers request review within 30 days. Credits are the sole financial remedy for an SLA miss unless
the contract states otherwise.

Contractual dashboard target: 99.5% monthly availability for X1 Pro.

title: GreenLoop Q3 FY2025 Report
text:
GREENLOOP ROBOTICS | Q3 FY2025 Business and Financial Report | | FIN-Q3-2025-v1.0

SECTION 1
Executive summary

GreenLoop delivered its strongest quarter to date, with revenue growth across hardware, cloud
subscriptions, and installation services. Product field quality and dashboard availability also
improved, although multi-item deposits and site conditions remained important support drivers.

Quarter in brief

● | Q3 revenue was USD 3.06 million, up USD 0.66 million or 27.5% from Q2 revenue of USD 2.40 million.

● | 455 Sorter X1 units were sold in Q3, compared with 320 in Q2.

● | Gross margin increased from 43% to 47% as supplier pricing and installation efficiency improved.

● | Fleet field classification accuracy increased from 87.9% to 89.1%.

● | Cloud dashboard availability was 99.72%, above the 99.5% X1 Pro SLA target.

● | Ending active customers increased from 190 to 238, while annual recurring revenue reached USD 2.87 million.

Management view

Growth was led by university and transport-hub programs in the Middle East and Europe. The Q3 result also
benefited from improved component availability. Operating loss narrowed, but the company remained
loss-making as it invested in model quality, service capacity, and the next hardware revision.

Q3 FY2025: USD 3.06M revenue, 27.5% sequential growth, 47% gross margin, 455 units sold,
and 99.72% dashboard availability.
